core_intuitions: The flywheel is a self-reinforcing loop whose returns COMPOUND: better data -> better model -> more users -> denser, sharper-tail signal -> better data. After k rounds an incumbent's training distribution covers a long tail no static dataset can match, and this distribution gap — not the model architecture — is the moat a new entrant cannot close.

Explicit human preference (thumbs) is scarce and high-quality (a few percent of traffic); implicit behavioral signals (copy, edit, regenerate, session continuation) are abundant but noisy. A proxy reward model trained to predict explicit thumbs from implicit signals extends usable labels from that few-percent tip to ~100% of traffic.

Active learning gets far more model improvement per label by choosing WHICH examples to annotate: uncertainty (low per-token logprob) finds where the model struggles, but uncertainty alone over-samples one redundant cluster — a diversity / core-set constraint spreads the budget across the hard tail so each label buys new coverage.

Client feedback signals arrive seconds-to-minutes AFTER the request, so an immutable log record must be enriched later by a session-windowed streaming join keyed on request_id; records with no signal are not wasted — they carry implicit weak-supervision labels.

Retraining must add new capability WITHOUT erasing old: blend new data with a frozen replay buffer at ratio rho (~0.3-0.5) — too little replay forgets, too much dilutes the new signal — and never ship without an eval gate that Goodhart-proofs the loop by requiring win-rate vs production, a regression suite, and safety checks all to pass.

existing.name: flywheel-six-stage-loop
existing.issue: Renders cleanly and correctly in both themes; the six stages (Serve -> Log -> Label/Score -> Retrain -> Eval-gate -> Deploy) form a clear closed cycle with good reading order. Conveys the CYCLE well but deliberately not the compounding/widening-gap dimension (that is a separate intuition, handled by a new figure). One faint imperfection: the LABEL/SCORE box uses a slightly different grey fill than the other five beige boxes, a minor inconsistency, and box fills do not adapt between light/dark — but both remain fully legible.
existing.improvement: Optional polish only: unify the LABEL/SCORE box fill with the other five stage boxes for visual consistency. Not required.

existing.name: flywheel-async-signal-join
existing.issue: Correct, uncluttered, and legible in both themes. Two input streams (serving-tier request_events, browser-sdk signal_events) key into a session-windowed join job that forks into an enriched curated table (matched) and an unenriched bucket (no signal, implicit weak-supervision) — exactly the async-join intuition. Solid metaphor with the dashed no-signal branch.

existing.name: flywheel-system-architecture
existing.issue: Accurate end-to-end services view: the real-time serving loop (Users -> Serving Tier -> Eval Gate -> Retrain DAG) on the left and the numbered 1-5 Offline Processing column on the right, wired by logs / training data / candidate / pass-new-model edges. Reads well in both themes. In dark mode the grey Offline-Processing container and the faint title are a touch low-contrast but still readable; not worth a redraw.

new_figures.name: preference-signal-pyramid
new_figures.concept: A pyramid of feedback: a scarce high-quality explicit-thumbs tip over an abundant noisy base of implicit behavioral signals, with a proxy reward model that lifts the implicit base up to labeled status so ~100% of traffic becomes usable training signal.
new_figures.rationale: The 'Preference Collection in Production' section's whole point is that explicit labels are expensive and sparse while implicit signals cover everything, and that a proxy reward model bridges the two to reach 100% coverage — a claim restated as a Key Takeaway. Prose lists the implicit signals in a table but never shows the VOLUME-vs-QUALITY inversion or the bridging move visually. This single frame makes the central preference-collection intuition click: why you bother with noisy signals at all, and what the proxy RM buys you.
new_figures.placement: Preference Collection in Production -> after the 'Structured preference from downstream behavior' subsection (the implicit-signals table and the proxy-reward-model sentence)
new_figures.brief: A static infographic (viewBox ~ 820x400) built around a horizontally-banded triangle/pyramid on the LEFT (~55% width) with an annotation column on the RIGHT. The pyramid is split into two vertical zones by a labeled dual axis: draw a thin up-arrow on the far left labeled 'QUALITY / label value ->' (high at top) and a thin down-arrow or widening cue labeled 'VOLUME of traffic ->' (grows toward the base) so the reader reads the inversion. THREE horizontal bands stacked: TOP narrow tip 'Explicit preference' with sub-label 'thumbs up/down, side-by-side A/B choice' and a small muted tag '~a few % of requests' (illustrative wording, NOT an invented benchmark — phrase as 'a few percent', no hard number); use v-accent for this tip. MIDDLE band 'Implicit signals' sub-label 'copy - edit - regenerate - continue - abandon'; wider, v-fill tone. BOTTOM widest band 'Raw unlabeled traffic' sub-label 'every request, no explicit feedback', muted v-grid tone. To the RIGHT of the pyramid draw a rounded box 'Proxy reward model' (v-accent border) with a caption 'trained to predict explicit thumbs from implicit signals'; from it draw a bold curved arrow that sweeps DOWN across the middle+bottom bands and back UP, labeled 'scores the ~95% with no explicit label', visually 'lifting' the implicit/raw bands up to the quality level of the tip. End that arrow at a small output chip on the right 'labeled signal ~= 100% of traffic'. Add one muted footnote line: 'noisy individually, strongly correlated in aggregate'. Theme-safe: all fills/strokes via v-* classes / var(), no hardcoded primary colors; keep every band label and the proxy-RM box fully inside the viewBox with clear gutters; reading order top-tip -> down the bands -> proxy RM lift -> 100% chip must be unambiguous. Use ONLY qualitative volume language ('a few percent', 'the remaining ~95%', 'nearly all'); invent no metrics.

new_figures.name: active-learning-uncertainty-vs-diversity
new_figures.concept: Same embedding space, two label-selection strategies: pure uncertainty sampling clumps the budget onto one redundant hard cluster, while uncertainty + core-set diversity spreads the same budget across the hard tail for far more coverage per label.
new_figures.rationale: 'Active Learning: which examples to label next' is the most label-efficiency-critical section, and its core lesson — uncertainty alone over-samples near-duplicates, so you add a diversity/core-set constraint — is inherently GEOMETRIC and does not survive as prose or code. A two-panel scatter contrasting the two selections makes the '3-5x more label-efficient' Key Takeaway visceral: the reader literally sees redundant picks vs spread picks. This is the figure that turns the coreset_sampler code into intuition.
new_figures.placement: Active Learning: Which Examples to Label Next? -> at the 'Core-set / diversity sampling' subsection
new_figures.brief: A static side-by-side two-panel infographic (viewBox ~ 840x400) sharing ONE identical scatter of prompt embeddings so the panels are directly comparable. Generate the SAME ~60-80 small dots laid out as a few loose visual CLUSTERS in a 2-D box (a plain unlabeled 2-D 'embedding space', axes drawn as faint corner ticks only, no numbers). Encode UNCERTAINTY as dot appearance: high-uncertainty dots are larger and filled v-accent, low-uncertainty dots are small and muted; arrange so ONE dense cluster is mostly high-uncertainty (the 'hard concept' clump) while other clusters have a scattering of moderately-uncertain dots. LEFT PANEL title 'Uncertainty only': ring/halo the ~10-12 SELECTED dots — all drawn from inside that single high-uncertainty cluster, packed tightly together; caption below 'picks the most-uncertain points -> many near-duplicates, one region covered'. RIGHT PANEL title 'Uncertainty + core-set diversity': ring the SAME budget of ~10-12 selected dots but now SPREAD one-per-cluster across the space, each ring landing on the most-uncertain member of its cluster; draw faint farthest-first / spacing guide lines or a light Voronoi-ish partition to hint the core-set construction; caption 'diversity constraint -> the budget covers the whole hard tail'. Put a short banner between/above the panels: 'same # of labels, very different coverage'. Optionally a tiny legend chip: large accent dot = 'high uncertainty', small muted dot = 'low uncertainty', ringed dot = 'chosen to label'. Theme-safe v-* classes / var() only; identical dot positions in both panels is essential; keep rings, guide lines, captions, and legend all inside the viewBox without overlapping the dots illegibly. Use NO invented efficiency numbers in the figure (the '3-5x' claim stays in the prose).

new_figures.name: compounding-flywheel-moat
new_figures.concept: Round by round, the incumbent's flywheel spins up — quality and data-tail coverage climb super-linearly while a new entrant starting from the same base model but no traffic stays flat — so the gap that opens is a data-distribution moat, not an architecture gap.
new_figures.rationale: Compounding is the chapter's OPENING thesis and its first Key Takeaway ('after enough rounds, the distribution gap is larger than any architectural advantage'), and the 'Compounding Data Advantage' section formalizes it with N_{k+1}, D_{k+1}, Q_{k+1} recurrences — but there is no visual. The six-stage-loop figure shows ONE turn of the cycle, not the widening trajectory over many turns. This figure supplies the missing dimension: WHY the loop is a moat. It converts the recurrence math into a diverging-curves intuition a reader remembers.
new_figures.placement: The Compounding Data Advantage -> after the recurrence definitions (N_{k+1}, D_{k+1}, Q_{k+1}) and the 'even with modest values ... after 10 rounds' line; also referenceable from 'Why the flywheel is a moat'
new_figures.brief: A static infographic (viewBox ~ 840x400) combining a small round-to-round loop motif with a divergence chart. LEFT (~40%): a compact upward SPIRAL of the flywheel drawn as 3-4 progressively LARGER loop icons stacked/ascending, each a mini closed arrow-ring labeled round k=1, k=2, k=3... and each annotated with a growing chip 'data D_k up', 'tail coverage up', 'quality Q_k up' so the reader sees each turn is bigger and denser than the last; a curved 'feeds' arrow from each loop up into the next. Small caption 'each turn: more users -> more signal -> sharper tail'. RIGHT (~60%): a line chart with x-axis 'flywheel rounds k' (ticks k=0..10, no other numbers) and y-axis 'model quality / tail coverage' (unlabeled scale, qualitative). Plot TWO trajectories: an INCUMBENT curve (v-accent, bold) rising with an accelerating / super-linear shape and a NEW-ENTRANT curve (muted/dashed) that starts at the SAME y as the incumbent's k=0 point (same base model) but stays nearly FLAT because it has no traffic. Shade the widening vertical band between the two curves with a light v-fill and label it 'the moat = distribution gap' with a small note 'closes via better data, not better architecture'. Mark the shared start point 'same open-weight base at k=0'. Add one muted annotation near the incumbent curve: 'user growth is multiplicative; quality grows with log(data)'. Theme-safe: v-* classes / var() only, no hardcoded primary colors; the two curves must be distinguishable in BOTH themes by shape+dash, not color alone; keep the spiral, both curves, the shaded moat band, and all labels inside the viewBox with no clipping. Axes carry NO invented numeric values — qualitative shape only.

summary: This chapter is concept-rich and its three existing figures are all clean, correct, and legible in both light and dark themes — but they are all STRUCTURAL (the six-stage cycle, the async-join topology, the full services architecture). They collectively answer 'what are the pieces and how are they wired', so all three are KEPT (only one optional cosmetic tweak: unify a mismatched box fill in the six-stage loop). What the figures do NOT convey is the INTUITION behind the three techniques the chapter argues make the flywheel work, plus its own headline thesis. I propose three disciplined static infographics, each tied to a stated core intuition: (1) a preference-signal pyramid showing the volume-vs-quality inversion and how a proxy reward model lifts abundant noisy implicit signals to ~100% labeled coverage (P1); (2) a two-panel embedding scatter contrasting uncertainty-only sampling (redundant clump) with uncertainty+core-set diversity (spread coverage), making the label-efficiency lesson geometric (P1); and (3) a compounding-moat figure pairing an ascending flywheel spiral with two diverging quality-vs-rounds trajectories (incumbent vs a flat new entrant from the same base), supplying the widening-gap dimension the single-turn loop figure cannot (P2). All three are static because one well-composed frame suffices for each; none need animation. Every brief is theme-safe (v-* classes only), uses qualitative volume/scale language with no invented benchmark numbers, and keeps all content inside the viewBox with explicit reading order.
